documents.title: ESM vs IIFE vs Legacy
headings: ESM vs IIFE vs Legacy Guide
documents.bodyText: JavaScript module formats can feel confusing — ESM, IIFE, CJS, UMD… what do these acronyms even mean? This guide breaks it all down in plain language, helps you pick the right one for your project, and shows you exactly how to set each up with Vanduo.. When you include JavaScript on a web page, the browser needs to know how that code is packaged. Think of module formats like different types of packaging for the same product:. Immediately Invoked Function Expression. The classic <script> tag approach. Drop it in, it just works. Creates a global Vanduo object.. ECMAScript Modules. The modern standard. Use import / export . Works with bundlers like Vite, Webpack, Rollup.. CommonJS. The Node.js format. Uses require() . For older build tools or server-side rendering.. Follow this decision tree to find the right format for your project:. Are you using a bundler? (Vite, Webpack, Rollup, Parcel, esbuild). Yes → Use ESM. You get tree-shaking (smaller bundles), modern syntax, and the best developer experience.. No → Next question.... Do you need to support very old browsers? (IE11, old Edge). Yes → Use CJS with a bundler/transpiler. No → Use IIFE. Best for: Static sites, prototypes, learning, WordPress themes, simple projects without a build step.. <!-- Add these before </body> --> < script src = "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/vanduo-oss/framework@v1.3.8/dist/vanduo.min.js" ></ script > < script > // Vanduo is now available as a global object Vanduo.init(); // Use any component Vanduo.components.toast.show({ title: 'Hello!', message: 'IIFE is working.', type: 'success' }); </ script > How it works: The browser downloads the script, executes it immediately, and attaches Vanduo to the global window object. Every component is included — you don't pick and choose.. Pros:. Cons:. Best for: Projects using Vite, Webpack, Rollup, or any modern bundler. SPAs, React/Vue/Svelte apps, production sites where bundle size matters.. // In your main JavaScript file (e.g., main.js or app.js) import { Vanduo } from './dist/vanduo.esm.js' ; // Or if installed via npm: // import { Vanduo } from '@vanduo-oss/framework'; // Initialize Vanduo. init (); // Use components with full IntelliSense support Vanduo.components.toast. show ({ title: 'Hello!' , message: 'ESM is working.' , type: 'success' }); To use ESM directly in a browser (without a bundler), you need type="module" :. < script type = "module" > import { Vanduo } from './dist/vanduo.esm.js'; Vanduo.init(); </ script > Watch out: type="module" scripts are deferred by default and have their own scope — you can't access variables from a module script in a regular script or inline onclick handlers. If you need global access, stick with IIFE. Pros:. Cons:. Best for: Node.js environments, server-side rendering (SSR), older bundlers that don't support ESM, legacy codebases being gradually modernized.. // CommonJS / require() syntax const { Vanduo } = require ( '@vanduo-oss/framework' ); // Or point directly to the CJS file: const { Vanduo } = require ( './dist/vanduo.cjs.js' ); Vanduo. init (); Watch out: CJS does not work in browsers without a bundler. If you're building a client-side website, use IIFE or ESM instead. CJS is primarily for server-side code and build pipelines. Pros:. Cons:. Here's the real-world difference — loading Vanduo with each format when you only need toasts and modals:. <!-- Loads ALL components --> < script src = "vanduo.min.js" ></ script > < script > Vanduo.init(); // You get toast + modal + every // other component whether you // use them or not </ script > Loads: ~45 KB gzipped (entire framework). // Only imports what you use import { Vanduo } from '@vanduo-oss/framework' ; Vanduo. init (); // Your bundler strips out unused // components automatically // (tree-shaking) Loads: Only the code you actually use. If you install Vanduo via npm, the package.json exports field automatically gives you the right format:. # Install npm install @vanduo-oss/framework # In your code: import { Vanduo } from '@vanduo-oss/framework' ; // → gets ESM const { Vanduo } = require ( '@vanduo-oss/framework' ); // → gets CJS Your bundler reads the "exports" field in package.json and selects the correct file automatically. You don't need to specify the file path.. Now that you know which module format to use, learn how the grid system works to structure your pages.. Using React, Vue, or Svelte? See how to wire Vanduo into your framework's lifecycle.. Zero configuration. Works everywhere. No mandatory build tools needed. Great for CDN delivery. Loads the entire library (no tree-shaking). One global variable (potential naming conflicts). No static analysis from your editor. Tree-shaking (smaller bundles). Better editor IntelliSense. Static analysis and linting. The web platform standard. Needs a bundler for best results. Slightly more setup. Module scope can surprise beginners. Works with Node.js natively. Compatible with older tooling. Synchronous loading. No tree-shaking. Not browser-native. Being superseded by ESM in Node.js. TL;DR for Beginners: If you are building a modern app with React, Vue, Next.js, or Vite, use ESM . If you are just building a simple HTML page and want to drop in a script tag to make components work immediately, use IIFE . You can safely ignore the rest!. Pro tip: If you're just getting started or building a simple site, IIFE is your best friend . No mandatory build tools, no complex configuration — just a <script> tag and you're done. You can always switch to ESM later when you adopt a bundler.. Watch out: type="module" scripts are deferred by default and have their own scope — you can't access variables from a module script in a regular script or inline onclick handlers. If you need global access, stick with IIFE.. Watch out: CJS does not work in browsers without a bundler. If you're building a client-side website, use IIFE or ESM instead. CJS is primarily for server-side code and build pipelines.. Still not sure? Start with IIFE . It works without any tooling, and you can always migrate to ESM later when your project grows. See the Module Formats reference in Concepts & Architecture for the full technical details.

chunks.heading: Format 1 IIFE — The Drop-In Approach

chunks.text: Best for: Projects using Vite, Webpack, Rollup, or any modern bundler. SPAs, React/Vue/Svelte apps, production sites where bundle size matters.
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: // In your main JavaScript file (e.g., main.js or app.js) import { Vanduo } from './dist/vanduo.esm.js' ; // Or if installed via npm: // import { Vanduo } from '@vanduo-oss/framework'; // Initialize Vanduo. init (); // Use components with full IntelliSense support Vanduo.components.toast. show ({ title: 'Hello!' , message: 'ESM is working.' , type: 'success' }); To use ESM directly in a browser (without a bundler), you need type="module" :
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: < script type = "module" > import { Vanduo } from './dist/vanduo.esm.js'; Vanduo.init(); </ script > Watch out: type="module" scripts are deferred by default and have their own scope — you can't access variables from a module script in a regular script or inline onclick handlers. If you need global access, stick with IIFE. Pros:
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Tree-shaking (smaller bundles)
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Better editor IntelliSense
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Static analysis and linting
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: The web platform standard
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Needs a bundler for best results
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Slightly more setup
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Module scope can surprise beginners
chunks.heading: Format 2 ESM — The Modern Standard

chunks.text: Best for: Node.js environments, server-side rendering (SSR), older bundlers that don't support ESM, legacy codebases being gradually modernized.
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: // CommonJS / require() syntax const { Vanduo } = require ( '@vanduo-oss/framework' ); // Or point directly to the CJS file: const { Vanduo } = require ( './dist/vanduo.cjs.js' ); Vanduo. init (); Watch out: CJS does not work in browsers without a bundler. If you're building a client-side website, use IIFE or ESM instead. CJS is primarily for server-side code and build pipelines. Pros:
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: Works with Node.js natively
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: Compatible with older tooling
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: Synchronous loading
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: No tree-shaking
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: Not browser-native
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: Being superseded by ESM in Node.js
chunks.heading: Format 3 CJS — The Legacy Path

chunks.text: Here's the real-world difference — loading Vanduo with each format when you only need toasts and modals:
chunks.heading: Before / After: Loading Components

chunks.text: <!-- Loads ALL components --> < script src = "vanduo.min.js" ></ script > < script > Vanduo.init(); // You get toast + modal + every // other component whether you // use them or not </ script > Loads: ~45 KB gzipped (entire framework)
chunks.heading: IIFE / CJS

chunks.text: // Only imports what you use import { Vanduo } from '@vanduo-oss/framework' ; Vanduo. init (); // Your bundler strips out unused // components automatically // (tree-shaking) Loads: Only the code you actually use
chunks.heading: ESM with Bundler

chunks.text: Setup complexity — Minimal — one script tag
chunks.heading: Cheat Sheet

chunks.text: Bundle size — Full library loaded
chunks.heading: Cheat Sheet

chunks.text: Browser support — All browsers
chunks.heading: Cheat Sheet

chunks.text: Editor IntelliSense — Limited
chunks.heading: Cheat Sheet

chunks.text: Best for — Simple sites, CDN, learning
chunks.heading: Cheat Sheet

chunks.text: File to use — vanduo.min.js
chunks.heading: Cheat Sheet

chunks.text: If you install Vanduo via npm, the package.json exports field automatically gives you the right format:
chunks.heading: For npm Users: Package Exports

chunks.text: # Install npm install @vanduo-oss/framework # In your code: import { Vanduo } from '@vanduo-oss/framework' ; // → gets ESM const { Vanduo } = require ( '@vanduo-oss/framework' ); // → gets CJS Your bundler reads the "exports" field in package.json and selects the correct file automatically. You don't need to specify the file path.
chunks.heading: For npm Users: Package Exports

documents.title: Building Your First Layout
headings: The Mental Model: Container → Row → Columns
documents.bodyText: Every web page needs structure — where does the sidebar go? How wide is the content area? How does it look on a phone? This guide teaches you Vanduo's grid system from the ground up, with live examples you can tweak and learn from.. Vanduo's grid has three layers. Think of it like a bookshelf:. < div class = "vd-container" > <!-- The bookshelf --> < div class = "vd-row" > <!-- A shelf --> < div class = "vd-col-6" >...</ div > <!-- Left half (6 of 12) --> < div class = "vd-col-6" >...</ div > <!-- Right half (6 of 12) --> </ div > </ div > Why 12 columns? Because 12 divides evenly by 2, 3, 4, and 6 — giving you maximum flexibility. Want 2 equal columns? 6+6. Three columns? 4+4+4. Four columns? 3+3+3+3. A sidebar + main? 3+9. Step 1 of 5 The Container Every layout starts with a container. You have two choices:. Fixed max-width, centered. Gets wider at each breakpoint. Best for most pages.. < div class = "vd-container" > <!-- Content is centered with max-width at each breakpoint --> </ div > vd-container-fluid Full width, always. Stretches to the edge of the viewport. Best for dashboards or wide layouts.. < div class = "vd-container-fluid" > <!-- Content stretches to fill the full viewport width --> </ div > Pro tip: You can also use responsive containers like vd-container-md or vd-container-lg that become fluid below their breakpoint. This gives you full-width on mobile and centered on desktop. Step 2 of 5 Rows and Columns Inside a container, create rows and fill them with columns. Here are the most common patterns:. < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-6" >Left half</ div > < div class = "vd-col-6" >Right half</ div > </ div > Three equal columns vd-col-4 vd-col-4 vd-col-4 View Code HTML CSS JS Copy < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-4" >One third</ div > < div class = "vd-col-4" >One third</ div > < div class = "vd-col-4" >One third</ div > </ div > Sidebar + Main content vd-col-3 (sidebar) vd-col-9 (main content) View Code HTML CSS JS Copy < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-3" >Sidebar</ div > < div class = "vd-col-9" >Main content</ div > </ div > Step 3 of 5 Responsive Breakpoints The real power of the grid is making layouts adapt to different screen sizes . Add a breakpoint suffix to your column classes:. Example: Full width on mobile, 2 columns on tablet, 3 columns on desktop:. < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-lg-4" >Item 1</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-lg-4" >Item 2</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-lg-4" >Item 3</ div > </ div > <!-- Mobile (0-767px): 1 column (vd-col-12 = full width each) Tablet (768-991px): 2 columns (vd-col-md-6 = half width each) Desktop (992px+): 3 columns (vd-col-lg-4 = one-third each) --> Pro tip: Always design mobile-first . Start with vd-col-12 (full width), then add breakpoint overrides like vd-col-md-6 and vd-col-lg-4 for wider screens. Step 4 of 5 Nesting Columns You can put a new vd-row inside any column to create sub-grids. This is how real-world layouts are built — a main structure with nested detail:. Nav links here. < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-3" > <!-- Sidebar --> < nav >...</ nav > </ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-9" > <!-- Main content with nested grid --> < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-6" >Card 1</ div > < div class = "vd-col-6" >Card 2</ div > </ div > </ div > </ div > Step 5 of 5 Offsets and Ordering Offsets push columns to the right by a number of column widths. Great for centering a narrow column:. < div class = "vd-row" > <!-- 3 columns of empty space + 6 columns of content + 3 remaining = centered --> < div class = "vd-col-6 vd-offset-3" >Centered content</ div > </ div > Ordering lets you rearrange columns without changing your HTML. Useful when you want a sidebar on the right on desktop but on top on mobile:. < div class = "vd-row" > <!-- On mobile: sidebar appears first (order-1), main second (order-2) On desktop: main appears first (md-order-1), sidebar second (md-order-2) --> < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-3 vd-order-1 vd-order-md-2" >Sidebar</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-9 vd-order-2 vd-order-md-1" >Main Content</ div > </ div > Common Layout Recipes Copy-paste these patterns to quickly build common page layouts:. < div class = "vd-container" > < div class = "vd-row" > < main class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-lg-8" > <!-- Blog posts go here --> </ main > < aside class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-lg-4" > <!-- Sidebar: recent posts, tags, about --> </ aside > </ div > </ div > Dashboard Layout View Code HTML CSS JS Copy < div class = "vd-container-fluid" > < div class = "vd-row" > < nav class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-lg-2" > <!-- Sidebar navigation --> </ nav > < main class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-lg-10" > < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-xl-3" >Stat Card 1</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-xl-3" >Stat Card 2</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-xl-3" >Stat Card 3</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-6 vd-col-xl-3" >Stat Card 4</ div > </ div > </ main > </ div > </ div > Landing Page View Code HTML CSS JS Copy < div class = "vd-container" > <!-- Hero: centered, narrow --> < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-8 vd-offset-md-2" style = "text-align: center;" > < h1 >Big headline</ h1 > < p >Subheading text</ p > < a href = "#" class = "vd-btn vd-btn-primary vd-btn-lg" >Call to Action</ a > </ div > </ div > <!-- Features: 3-column grid --> < div class = "vd-row vd-mt-13" > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-4" >Feature 1</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-4" >Feature 2</ div > < div class = "vd-col-12 vd-col-md-4" >Feature 3</ div > </ div > </ div > Pro tip: Open your browser's DevTools (F12) and toggle the responsive design mode to see your layout adapt in real time. This is the best way to learn how breakpoints feel. What's Next? Fibonacci vs Standard Grid Discover the design theory behind Vanduo's unique responsive sizing.. Quick reference for all spacing, typography, and layout classes.. Container — The bookshelf itself. Centers your content and sets the maximum width.. Row — A shelf. Creates a horizontal strip that holds columns.. Columns — The books on the shelf. Each row splits into 12 equal columns , and you decide how many each piece of content gets.. Why 12 columns? Because 12 divides evenly by 2, 3, 4, and 6 — giving you maximum flexibility. Want 2 equal columns? 6+6. Three columns? 4+4+4. Four columns? 3+3+3+3. A sidebar + main? 3+9.. Pro tip: You can also use responsive containers like vd-container-md or vd-container-lg that become fluid below their breakpoint. This gives you full-width on mobile and centered on desktop.. Pro tip: Always design mobile-first . Start with vd-col-12 (full width), then add breakpoint overrides like vd-col-md-6 and vd-col-lg-4 for wider screens.. Pro tip: Open your browser's DevTools (F12) and toggle the responsive design mode to see your layout adapt in real time. This is the best way to learn how breakpoints feel.

documents.title: Fibonacci vs Standard Grid
headings: Step 4 of 4 The Grid Mode Toggle
documents.bodyText: Most CSS frameworks give you equal columns — 4+4+4 or 6+6. Vanduo goes further with Fibonacci-based layouts that follow the proportions found in nature: 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13… These ratios create visual harmony that feels "right" even if you can't explain why. Let's explore the difference.. Each number is the sum of the two before it:. As the numbers grow, the ratio between consecutive numbers approaches ~1.618 — the Golden Ratio (φ). This ratio appears in sunflowers, seashells, galaxies, and the proportions of classical architecture.. Equal widths: 33% + 33% + 33%. Balanced, but uniform — every section gets the same emphasis.. Fibonacci widths: ~19% + ~31% + ~50%. Natural hierarchy — your eye moves from small to large, creating visual flow.. Vanduo provides ready-made Fibonacci layouts. Just swap your row class:. < div class = "vd-row-fib-4" > < div >Narrow (1 part)</ div > < div >Small (2 parts)</ div > < div >Medium (3 parts)</ div > < div >Wide (5 parts)</ div > </ div > 3-Column Fibonacci (3:5:8) 3 5 8 View Code HTML CSS JS Copy < div class = "vd-row-fib-3-alt" > < div >Sidebar (3 parts)</ div > < div >Secondary (5 parts)</ div > < div >Main content (8 parts)</ div > </ div > Pro tip: Fibonacci layouts are great for content-heavy pages — blog layouts, article pages, portfolios. The widest column naturally draws the eye to your main content. Step 2 of 4 CSS Grid Fibonacci Prefer CSS Grid over Flexbox? Vanduo has you covered with vd-grid-fib-* classes that use grid-template-columns with fractional units:. <!-- CSS Grid: 4-column Fibonacci (1fr 2fr 3fr 5fr) --> < div class = "vd-grid-fib-4" > < div >1fr</ div > < div >2fr</ div > < div >3fr</ div > < div >5fr</ div > </ div > <!-- CSS Grid: 3-column Fibonacci (3fr 5fr 8fr) --> < div class = "vd-grid-fib-3-alt" > < div >3fr</ div > < div >5fr</ div > < div >8fr</ div > </ div > Flexbox vs CSS Grid? Both give you Fibonacci proportions. Use vd-row-fib-* (Flexbox) when you want wrapping behavior on small screens. Use vd-grid-fib-* (CSS Grid) when you want strict column placement and alignment across rows. Step 3 of 4 Fibonacci Gap Utilities Fibonacci isn't just for column widths — Vanduo also provides Fibonacci-based spacing between grid items:. 3px gap — tight. 8px gap — comfortable. 21px gap — spacious. <!-- Fibonacci gaps --> < div class = "vd-row vd-gap-fib-3" >...</ div > <!-- 3px gap --> < div class = "vd-row vd-gap-fib-5" >...</ div > <!-- 5px gap --> < div class = "vd-row vd-gap-fib-8" >...</ div > <!-- 8px gap --> < div class = "vd-row vd-gap-fib-13" >...</ div > <!-- 13px gap --> < div class = "vd-row vd-gap-fib-21" >...</ div > <!-- 21px gap --> Step 4 of 4 The Grid Mode Toggle Here's something unique to Vanduo: you can let users switch between Standard and Fibonacci grid at runtime with a toggle button. Great for dashboards or design tools where users want to control the layout:. <!-- Add this button anywhere in your page --> < button data-grid-toggle aria-label = "Toggle grid mode" > < i class = "ph ph-grid-four" ></ i > Toggle Grid Mode </ button > <!-- Mark the section that should switch --> < div data-layout-mode class = "vd-container" > < div class = "vd-row" > < div class = "vd-col-4" >Column 1</ div > < div class = "vd-col-4" >Column 2</ div > < div class = "vd-col-4" >Column 3</ div > </ div > </ div > When the toggle is clicked, Vanduo swaps the grid from equal columns to Fibonacci proportions. The data-layout-mode attribute on the container tells Vanduo which section to transform.. See how to visually build a theme tailored perfectly for your brand.. Dive deeper into how Vanduo's variable tiers work and how to create custom themes.. Building data-heavy dashboards (equal emphasis on all metrics). Creating forms with aligned columns. E-commerce product grids (uniform card sizes). Any layout where symmetry is desired. Designing content-first pages (blogs, articles, portfolios). Creating visual hierarchy (main content + sidebar). Marketing / landing pages (guide the eye to the CTA). Any layout where you want one area to dominate. Why does this matter for web design? Our brains are wired to find Golden Ratio proportions pleasing. Using Fibonacci ratios in your layouts creates a subtle sense of balance and sophistication that equal-width columns simply can't achieve.. Pro tip: Fibonacci layouts are great for content-heavy pages — blog layouts, article pages, portfolios. The widest column naturally draws the eye to your main content.. Flexbox vs CSS Grid? Both give you Fibonacci proportions. Use vd-row-fib-* (Flexbox) when you want wrapping behavior on small screens. Use vd-grid-fib-* (CSS Grid) when you want strict column placement and alignment across rows.. Watch out: The grid toggle works on vd-row elements inside the data-layout-mode container. Make sure your columns add up to 12 in standard mode — Fibonacci mode recalculates proportions automatically.. Pro tip: You can mix both in the same page! Use a standard grid for your header and footer, and Fibonacci for the main content area. There's no rule against combining them.

documents.title: CSS Variables & Theming
headings: How Dark Mode Works Under the Hood
documents.bodyText: Vanduo is built on top of a powerful CSS variables (Custom Properties) foundation. This means you can fundamentally alter the look and feel of the entire framework just by changing a few variables in your root CSS file, without ever needing a preprocessor like Sass.. Vanduo's variables are layered to give you massive control with minimal effort:. To change Vanduo's default blue to your brand's purple, simply redefine --color-primary on the :root element in your own CSS file. Ensure your CSS is loaded after Vanduo's CSS.. :root { /* Change the primary brand color to Brand Purple */ --color-primary: #8a2be2; /* Buttons and links automatically inherit this change! */ /* You can also adjust foundational spacing */ --grid-gutter: 2rem; --btn-border-radius: 9999px; /* Pill-shaped buttons globally */ } How Dark Mode Works Under the Hood Unlike frameworks that require a separate CSS file or write heavy contextual selectors like .dark .btn { ... } , Vanduo handles dark mode purely by re-assigning semantic color variables at the root level.. When the data-theme="dark" attribute is added to the <html> tag, Vanduo swaps the literal hex values assigned to structural variables like --bg-primary and --text-primary .. /* By default (Light Mode) */ :root { --bg-primary: #ffffff; --text-primary: #1e293b; --border-color: #e2e8f0; } /* When Dark Mode is toggled on */ html[data-theme="dark"] { --bg-primary: #0f172a; /* Background becomes very dark blue */ --text-primary: #f8fafc; /* Text becomes near white */ --border-color: #334155; /* Borders become dark */ } /* Components just use the semantic variables. They don't care if it's light or dark! */ .vd-card { background-color: var(--bg-primary); color: var(--text-primary); border: 1px solid var(--border-color); } Because of this architecture, you can easily create custom themes (like "High Contrast" or "Sepia") by applying a new data attribute like data-theme="sepia" and overriding those same 5-6 semantic variables! Component-Level Tokens Beyond the global theme colors, Vanduo exposes scoped variables for individual components. These let you fine-tune a single component without affecting the rest of the system.. :root { /* Make all cards more rounded with extra padding */ --card-border-radius: 1rem; --card-padding: 2rem; /* Pill-shaped inputs */ --input-border-radius: 9999px; /* Taller navbar */ --navbar-height: 4.5rem; } How do I find all available tokens? Open your browser DevTools, select any Vanduo element, and look at the Computed tab — all CSS custom properties used by that element are listed there with their current values. Creating a Custom Theme File The recommended approach is to keep your overrides in a dedicated CSS file loaded after Vanduo's stylesheet. This keeps your customizations organized and easy to swap.. <!-- Vanduo first --> <link rel="stylesheet" href="vanduo/dist/vanduo.min.css"> <!-- Your overrides second (wins due to cascade order) --> <link rel="stylesheet" href="css/my-theme.css"> View Code css/my-theme.css Copy /* ── Brand Colors ──────────────────────────── */ :root { --color-primary: #6d28d9; --color-primary-hover: #5b21b6; --color-success: #059669; --color-danger: #dc2626; } /* ── Typography ───────────────────────────── */ :root { --font-family-sans: 'Inter', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, sans-serif; --font-family-mono: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; } /* ── Component Tuning ─────────────────────── */ :root { --btn-border-radius: 0.5rem; --card-border-radius: 0.75rem; --input-border-radius: 0.5rem; } /* ── Dark Mode Adjustments ────────────────── */ html[data-theme="dark"] { --bg-primary: #18181b; --bg-secondary: #27272a; --border-color: #3f3f46; } Creating a Fully Custom Theme You can go beyond light and dark by creating entirely new themes. Add a data-theme attribute to <html> and define the corresponding variables:. /* Sepia Theme */ html[data-theme="sepia"] { --bg-primary: #fdf6e3; --bg-secondary: #eee8d5; --text-primary: #657b83; --text-secondary: #586e75; --border-color: #d6cdb5; --color-primary: #b58900; --color-primary-hover: #a67c00; } /* High Contrast Theme */ html[data-theme="high-contrast"] { --bg-primary: #000000; --bg-secondary: #1a1a1a; --text-primary: #ffffff; --text-secondary: #e0e0e0; --border-color: #ffffff; --color-primary: #ffff00; --color-primary-hover: #cccc00; } Sepia Theme Warm and easy on the eyes.. Maximum legibility for visual impairments.. Then toggle between themes with JavaScript:. function setTheme(name) { document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', name); localStorage.setItem('vanduo-theme-preference', name); } // Usage setTheme('sepia'); setTheme('high-contrast'); setTheme('dark'); setTheme('light'); Practical Override Recipes Common real-world customizations you can copy and adapt:. Use these variables for a softer, friendlier look:. :root { --btn-border-radius: 9999px; --card-border-radius: 1.5rem; --input-border-radius: 9999px; --modal-border-radius: 1.5rem; } Corporate / Enterprise Feel A more formal, professional aesthetic:. Generate new API key for the production environment.. :root { --color-primary: #1e40af; --font-family-sans: 'Noto Sans', -apple-system, sans-serif; --btn-border-radius: 0.25rem; --card-border-radius: 0.375rem; --card-padding: 1.5rem; } Scoped Override (Single Page) You can scope overrides to a specific page or section by targeting a parent class or ID instead of :root :. .landing-page { --color-primary: #059669; --card-border-radius: 2rem; --btn-border-radius: 9999px; } .dashboard { --color-primary: #6366f1; --card-padding: 1rem; --card-border-radius: 0.375rem; } .landing-page Call to Action .dashboard View Report What's Next? Accessibility Essentials Learn how Vanduo handles accessibility out of the box, and best practices for inclusion.. Apply your custom variables at runtime with the visual theme customizer panel.. 1. Theme Colors: The highest level. Defines what "Primary" or "Success" actually means.. 2. Component Tokens: Variables scoped to specific components (e.g., --btn-height or --card-bg ).. 3. Utility Variables: Core infrastructure like spacing steps and border radii.. Because of this architecture, you can easily create custom themes (like "High Contrast" or "Sepia") by applying a new data attribute like data-theme="sepia" and overriding those same 5-6 semantic variables!. How do I find all available tokens? Open your browser DevTools, select any Vanduo element, and look at the Computed tab — all CSS custom properties used by that element are listed there with their current values.

chunks.text: Vanduo is built on top of a powerful CSS variables (Custom Properties) foundation. This means you can fundamentally alter the look and feel of the entire framework just by changing a few variables in your root CSS file, without ever needing a preprocessor like Sass.
chunks.heading: CSS Variables & Theming Guide

chunks.text: Vanduo's variables are layered to give you massive control with minimal effort:
chunks.heading: The Three Variable Tiers

chunks.text: 1. Theme Colors: The highest level. Defines what "Primary" or "Success" actually means.
chunks.heading: The Three Variable Tiers

chunks.text: 2. Component Tokens: Variables scoped to specific components (e.g., --btn-height or --card-bg ).
chunks.heading: The Three Variable Tiers

chunks.text: 3. Utility Variables: Core infrastructure like spacing steps and border radii.
chunks.heading: The Three Variable Tiers

chunks.text: To change Vanduo's default blue to your brand's purple, simply redefine --color-primary on the :root element in your own CSS file. Ensure your CSS is loaded after Vanduo's CSS.
chunks.heading: Overriding the Global Theme

chunks.text: Unlike frameworks that require a separate CSS file or write heavy contextual selectors like .dark .btn { ... } , Vanduo handles dark mode purely by re-assigning semantic color variables at the root level.
chunks.heading: How Dark Mode Works Under the Hood

chunks.text: When the data-theme="dark" attribute is added to the <html> tag, Vanduo swaps the literal hex values assigned to structural variables like --bg-primary and --text-primary .
chunks.heading: How Dark Mode Works Under the Hood

chunks.text: Beyond the global theme colors, Vanduo exposes scoped variables for individual components. These let you fine-tune a single component without affecting the rest of the system.
chunks.heading: Component-Level Tokens

chunks.text: Buttons — --btn-height , --btn-border-radius , --btn-font-weight
chunks.heading: Component-Level Tokens

chunks.text: Cards — --card-bg , --card-border-radius , --card-padding
chunks.heading: Component-Level Tokens

chunks.text: Navbar — --navbar-height , --navbar-bg
chunks.heading: Component-Level Tokens

chunks.text: Forms — --input-border-radius , --input-border-color , --input-bg
chunks.heading: Component-Level Tokens

chunks.text: Modal — --modal-bg , --modal-backdrop , --modal-border-radius
chunks.heading: Component-Level Tokens

chunks.text: The recommended approach is to keep your overrides in a dedicated CSS file loaded after Vanduo's stylesheet. This keeps your customizations organized and easy to swap.
chunks.heading: Creating a Custom Theme File

chunks.text: You can go beyond light and dark by creating entirely new themes. Add a data-theme attribute to <html> and define the corresponding variables:
chunks.heading: Creating a Fully Custom Theme

chunks.text: /* Sepia Theme */ html[data-theme="sepia"] { --bg-primary: #fdf6e3; --bg-secondary: #eee8d5; --text-primary: #657b83; --text-secondary: #586e75; --border-color: #d6cdb5; --color-primary: #b58900; --color-primary-hover: #a67c00; } /* High Contrast Theme */ html[data-theme="high-contrast"] { --bg-primary: #000000; --bg-secondary: #1a1a1a; --text-primary: #ffffff; --text-secondary: #e0e0e0; --border-color: #ffffff; --color-primary: #ffff00; --color-primary-hover: #cccc00; } Sepia Theme Warm and easy on the eyes.

documents.title: Theme Customizer Walkthrough
headings: Step 1 of 6 Add the Trigger Button
Step 4 of 6 Read the User's Choices
documents.bodyText: Vanduo's Theme Customizer is a floating panel that lets you — and your users — change the entire look and feel of a site at runtime: primary color, neutral palette, border radius, font family, and dark/light mode. This guide walks you through setting it up, reading values, reacting to changes, and building a fully branded experience.. The Theme Customizer gives your site a floating settings panel with these controls:. Sky, blue, indigo, violet, fuchsia, rose, red, orange, amber, yellow, lime, green, emerald, teal, cyan, slate, zinc, stone. Slate, gray, zinc, neutral, stone — controls backgrounds, text, and borders. Border radius presets, font family selection, and dark/light mode toggle. All changes persist in localStorage — your users' preferences survive page reloads and browser restarts.. The customizer needs a button for users to open it. Place this anywhere — in your navbar, a floating corner, or a settings page:. < button class = "vd-theme-customizer-trigger" data-theme-customizer-trigger aria-label = "Open theme customizer" > < i class = "ph ph-paint-roller" ></ i > </ button > How it works: The data-theme-customizer-trigger attribute tells Vanduo's JS to listen for clicks on this button and open the customizer panel.. The Theme Customizer is part of Vanduo's JS bundle — no extra files needed. Just make sure you have:. <!-- CSS (required) --> < link rel = "stylesheet" href = "path/to/vanduo.min.css" > <!-- JS (required) --> < script src = "path/to/vanduo.min.js" ></ script > < script > Vanduo.init(); // Initializes ALL components, including Theme Customizer </ script > That's it! Vanduo.init() scans the DOM, finds the trigger button, and sets up the customizer panel automatically.. Open the customizer and explore what each control does:. Need to know what the user selected? Use the getState() API:. // Get the current customizer state const state = Vanduo.components.themeCustomizer. getState (); console. log (state); // { // primary: 'blue', // neutral: 'slate', // radius: 'md', // font: 'Inter', // theme: 'dark' // } You can also read individual localStorage keys directly:. const primaryColor = localStorage. getItem ( 'vanduo-primary' ); // 'blue' const currentTheme = localStorage. getItem ( 'vanduo-theme-preference' ); // 'dark' Step 5 of 6 Set Values Programmatically You can control the customizer from your own code — perfect for "preset themes" or onboarding flows:. const tc = Vanduo.components.themeCustomizer; // Set individual values tc. setPrimary ( 'rose' ); // Change primary color tc. setNeutral ( 'zinc' ); // Change neutral palette tc. setRadius ( 'lg' ); // Change border radius tc. setFont ( 'Rubik' ); // Change font family tc. setTheme ( 'dark' ); // Switch to dark mode // Open/close the panel tc. open (); tc. close (); tc. toggle (); // Reset everything to defaults tc. reset (); Example: Preset Theme Buttons Give users quick-pick options for common themes:. < button onclick = "applyPreset('corporate')" >Corporate</ button > < button onclick = "applyPreset('playful')" >Playful</ button > < button onclick = "applyPreset('minimal')" >Minimal</ button > < script > function applyPreset(name) { const tc = Vanduo.components.themeCustomizer; const presets = { corporate: { primary: 'blue', neutral: 'slate', radius: 'sm', font: 'Inter' }, playful: { primary: 'fuchsia', neutral: 'stone', radius: 'xl', font: 'Rubik' }, minimal: { primary: 'zinc', neutral: 'neutral', radius: 'none', font: 'Source Sans' } }; const p = presets[name]; tc.setPrimary(p.primary); tc.setNeutral(p.neutral); tc.setRadius(p.radius); tc.setFont(p.font); } </ script > Step 6 of 6 Listen to Changes React to user customizations in real time with custom events:. // Fired whenever any setting changes document. addEventListener ( 'themeCustomizer:change' , (e) => { console. log ( 'Setting changed: ' , e.detail); // e.detail = { property: 'primary', value: 'rose' } }); // Fired when the panel opens document. addEventListener ( 'themeCustomizer: open' , () => { console. log ( 'Customizer panel opened' ); }); // Fired when the panel closes document. addEventListener ( 'themeCustomizer: close' , () => { console. log ( 'Customizer panel closed' ); }); // Fired when user clicks "Reset" document. addEventListener ( 'themeCustomizer: reset' , () => { console. log ( 'All settings reset to defaults' ); }); Pro tip: Use the themeCustomizer:change event to sync changes to your backend, apply analytics tracking, or update components that need to adapt to theme changes (like charts or maps). Before / After: Building a Brand See the difference a few customizer calls make:. // No customization Vanduo. init (); Blue primary, slate neutrals, medium radius, Inter font, light mode. Clean and professional, but generic.. Vanduo. init (); const tc = Vanduo.components.themeCustomizer; tc. setPrimary ( 'orange' ); tc. setNeutral ( 'stone' ); tc. setRadius ( 'lg' ); tc. setFont ( 'Rubik' ); Orange primary, warm stone neutrals, rounded corners, friendly Rubik font. Feels like a completely different product — 4 lines of code.. Need to go back to square one?. // Reset all customizer settings to defaults Vanduo.components.themeCustomizer. reset (); // This clears all 5 localStorage keys and // reverts the site to "default appearance Watch out: reset() clears all 5 customizer localStorage keys. If you've stored other data in localStorage, those are safe — only the vanduo-* keys are affected. You can also customize the panel's appearance itself with CSS variables:. :root { --tc-panel-bg : var ( --bg-primary ); --tc-panel-border : var ( --border-color ); --tc-panel-shadow : var ( --shadow-lg ); --tc-panel-width : 320 px ; --tc-panel-z-index : 9999 ; } What's Next? CSS Variables & Theming Learn how Vanduo's styling system works under the hood and how to override it via CSS.. Quick reference for all spacing, typography, color, and layout utility classes.. Pro tip: The vd-theme-customizer-trigger class provides default floating-button styling (fixed position, bottom-right corner). You can omit this class and use your own styling if you want the trigger in a specific location.. Why localStorage? When users pick a theme, it should stick between visits. The customizer saves each choice to localStorage under the keys above, and restores them automatically on page load.. Pro tip: Use the themeCustomizer:change event to sync changes to your backend, apply analytics tracking, or update components that need to adapt to theme changes (like charts or maps).. Watch out: reset() clears all 5 customizer localStorage keys. If you've stored other data in localStorage, those are safe — only the vanduo-* keys are affected.

documents.bodyText:
Vanduo includes a comprehensive set of utility classes based on the Fibonacci scale (8, 13, 21, 34, 55...). This cheat sheet provides a quick reference for all available utilities.. Format: vd-{property}-{direction}-{size}.

Card Title

Content with controlled spacing.

Typography Class Description vd-text-left Text align left vd-text-center Text align center vd-text-right Text align right vd-text-sm Small text (0.875rem) vd-text-base Base text (1rem) vd-text-lg Large text (1.25rem) vd-font-normal Font weight 400 vd-font-medium Font weight 500 vd-font-bold Font weight 700 HTML

Centered Bold Heading

Large muted subtitle text

Small medium-weight caption Colors & Backgrounds Use these classes to quickly apply themed colors to text or backgrounds..
Left content Right content

Title

Subtitle

Desktop-only sidebar
Radius & Visuals Class Description vd-rounded-none Border radius 0 vd-rounded-sm Small border radius (4px) vd-rounded-md Medium border radius (8px) vd-rounded-lg Large border radius (12px) vd-rounded-full Full border radius (9999px) vd-shadow-sm Small drop shadow vd-shadow-md Medium drop shadow vd-shadow-lg Large drop shadow HTML
Elevated content
NEW What's Next? CSS Variables & Theming Learn how Vanduo's styling system works under the hood and how to override it.. Best practices for inclusive design — keyboard navigation, screen readers, and contrast.

documents.title: Accessibility Essentials
headings: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free
documents.bodyText: Accessibility isn't a feature you bolt on at the end — it's about making sure everyone can use what you build. People with visual impairments, motor disabilities, cognitive differences, or temporary injuries (a broken arm, bright sunlight on a screen) all benefit from accessible design. This guide shows you what Vanduo handles for you, and what you need to do yourself.. have some form of disability. That's 25% of your potential users.. Captions help in noisy rooms. High contrast helps in sunlight. Keyboard nav helps power users.. Laws like ADA, Section 508, and EAA mandate accessibility for many websites.. Good news — Vanduo's components are built with accessibility in mind. Here's what you get out of the box:. Many users navigate with keyboards alone — Tab to move between elements, Enter/Space to activate, Escape to close. Here's how to ensure your site works without a mouse:. By default, browsers tab through elements in DOM order. If your visual layout differs from the source order (e.g., using CSS Grid reordering), users may get confused. Use tabindex sparingly and only when necessary:. < button tabindex = "3" >Third < button tabindex = "1" >First < button tabindex = "2" >Second < button >First < button >Second < button >Third Skip-to-Content Link Help keyboard users skip past the navbar to the main content:. < a href = "#main-content" class = "vd-skip-link" >Skip to main content < nav class = "vd-navbar" >... < main id = "main-content" > Pro tip: Test your entire site using only the keyboard. Unplug your mouse (or just don't touch it) and try to complete every user action. You'll immediately find problems — elements you can't reach, invisible focus states, trapped focus. Essential 2 Screen Reader Essentials Screen readers convert your page into speech or Braille. They rely on HTML structure and ARIA attributes to convey meaning. Here's what you need to add yourself:. < button > < i class = "ph ph-x" > < img src = "hero.jpg" > < input type = "text" > Users have no idea what these elements do.. < button aria-label = "Close dialog" > < i class = "ph ph-x" > < img src = "hero.jpg" alt = "Sunset over the ocean" > < label for = "email" >Email address < input type = "text" id = "email" > Every element communicates its purpose.. < div onclick = "doSomething()" >Click me < button onclick = "doSomething()" >Click me Essential 3 Color and Contrast Text needs enough contrast against its background to be readable. WCAG requires:. Never rely on color alone to convey information:. < p style = "color: red;" > Invalid email Color + Icon + Text View Code HTML CSS JS Copy < div class = "vd-alert vd-alert-danger" > < i class = "ph ph-warning-circle" > < strong >Error: Please enter a valid email address. Essential 4 Forms Accessibility Forms are where accessibility creates or breaks the experience. Every input field needs a proper label, and errors need to be communicated clearly:. < div class = "vd-form-group" > < label for = "user-email" >Email Address < input type = "email" id = "user-email" class = "vd-input" aria-describedby = "email-error" aria-invalid = "true" placeholder = "you@example.com" > < p id = "email-error" class = "vd-form-error" role = "alert" > < i class = "ph ph-warning-circle" > Please enter a valid email address. Key patterns:. You don't need to be an accessibility expert to test your site. Here are the most effective approaches, from easiest to most thorough:. Don't touch your mouse. Tab through every page. Can you reach everything? Can you see where focus is? Can you activate buttons with Enter?. Time: 5 minutes per page. Chrome's Lighthouse (Audits tab) has an Accessibility score. Run it and aim for 90+. It catches missing labels, low contrast, and missing ARIA.. Time: 1 minute per page. A free browser extension that finds accessibility issues and explains how to fix them. Much more detailed than Lighthouse.. Time: 2-3 minutes per page. Try VoiceOver (macOS: Cmd+F5), NVDA (Windows, free), or TalkBack (Android). Listen to your site — does it make sense?. Time: 10-15 minutes (first time). Bookmark this quick-reference checklist for every page you build:. Learn how to boost page performance by deferring section contents until they are scrolled into view.. Customize Vanduo's look by overriding CSS variables, and ensure contrast ratios stay accessible.. AA (minimum): 4.5:1 ratio for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18px+ bold or 24px+). AAA (enhanced): 7:1 ratio for normal text, 4.5:1 for large text. for="id" on

chunks.text: Accessibility isn't a feature you bolt on at the end — it's about making sure everyone can use what you build. People with visual impairments, motor disabilities, cognitive differences, or temporary injuries (a broken arm, bright sunlight on a screen) all benefit from accessible design. This guide shows you what Vanduo handles for you, and what you need to do yourself.
chunks.heading: Accessibility Essentials Guide

chunks.text: have some form of disability. That's 25% of your potential users.
chunks.heading: 1 in 4 Adults

chunks.text: Captions help in noisy rooms. High contrast helps in sunlight. Keyboard nav helps power users.
chunks.heading: It Helps Everyone

chunks.text: Laws like ADA, Section 508, and EAA mandate accessibility for many websites.
chunks.heading: It's Often Required

chunks.text: Good news — Vanduo's components are built with accessibility in mind. Here's what you get out of the box:
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: ARIA attributes — Modals, tooltips, dropdowns, and collapsibles get aria-expanded , aria-hidden , and role attributes automatically via JS
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: Focus management — Modals trap focus inside them. When you close a modal, focus returns to the trigger element
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: Focus-visible styles — Keyboard users see clear focus rings, while mouse users don't — using :focus-visible
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: Color contrast — The default theme meets WCAG AA contrast ratios for text and interactive elements
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: Semantic HTML — Components use proper elements: <button> for actions, <nav> for navigation, <table> for data
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: Keyboard interaction — Escape closes modals/dropdowns, Enter/Space activates buttons, arrow keys navigate tabs
chunks.heading: Built-in What Vanduo Gives You for Free

chunks.text: Many users navigate with keyboards alone — Tab to move between elements, Enter/Space to activate, Escape to close. Here's how to ensure your site works without a mouse:
chunks.heading: Essential 1 Keyboard Navigation

chunks.text: By default, browsers tab through elements in DOM order. If your visual layout differs from the source order (e.g., using CSS Grid reordering), users may get confused. Use tabindex sparingly and only when necessary:
chunks.heading: Tab Order

chunks.text: Help keyboard users skip past the navbar to the main content:
chunks.heading: Skip-to-Content Link

chunks.text: Screen readers convert your page into speech or Braille. They rely on HTML structure and ARIA attributes to convey meaning. Here's what you need to add yourself:
chunks.heading: Essential 2 Screen Reader Essentials

chunks.text: <!-- Screen reader says: "Button" --> < button > < i class = "ph ph-x" ></ i > </ button > <!-- Screen reader says: "Image" --> < img src = "hero.jpg" > <!-- Screen reader says: "Edit text" --> < input type = "text" > Users have no idea what these elements do.
chunks.heading: Without Labels

chunks.text: <!-- "Close dialog" --> < button aria-label = "Close dialog" > < i class = "ph ph-x" ></ i > </ button > <!-- "Sunset over the ocean" --> < img src = "hero.jpg" alt = "Sunset over the ocean" > <!-- "Email address, edit text" --> < label for = "email" >Email address</ label > < input type = "text" id = "email" > Every element communicates its purpose.
chunks.heading: With Labels

chunks.text: Text needs enough contrast against its background to be readable. WCAG requires:
chunks.heading: Essential 3 Color and Contrast

chunks.text: Never rely on color alone to convey information:
chunks.heading: Essential 3 Color and Contrast

chunks.text: AA (minimum): 4.5:1 ratio for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18px+ bold or 24px+)
chunks.heading: Essential 3 Color and Contrast

chunks.text: AAA (enhanced): 7:1 ratio for normal text, 4.5:1 for large text
chunks.heading: Essential 3 Color and Contrast

chunks.text: Forms are where accessibility creates or breaks the experience. Every input field needs a proper label, and errors need to be communicated clearly:
chunks.heading: Essential 4 Forms Accessibility

chunks.text: <!-- Accessible form field with error --> < div class = "vd-form-group" > < label for = "user-email" >Email Address</ label > < input type = "email" id = "user-email" class = "vd-input" aria-describedby = "email-error" aria-invalid = "true" placeholder = "you@example.com" > < p id = "email-error" class = "vd-form-error" role = "alert" > < i class = "ph ph-warning-circle" ></ i > Please enter a valid email address. </ p > </ div > Key patterns:
chunks.heading: Essential 4 Forms Accessibility

chunks.text: for="id" on <label> connects it to the input — clicking the label focuses the input
chunks.heading: Essential 4 Forms Accessibility

chunks.text: aria-describedby connects the input to its error message — screen readers read both
chunks.heading: Essential 4 Forms Accessibility

chunks.text: aria-invalid="true" tells screen readers this field has an error
chunks.heading: Essential 4 Forms Accessibility

chunks.text: role="alert" on the error message makes screen readers announce it immediately when it appears
chunks.heading: Essential 4 Forms Accessibility

chunks.text: You don't need to be an accessibility expert to test your site. Here are the most effective approaches, from easiest to most thorough:
chunks.heading: Essential 5 Testing Your Work

chunks.text: Don't touch your mouse. Tab through every page. Can you reach everything? Can you see where focus is? Can you activate buttons with Enter?
chunks.heading: 1. Keyboard Test

chunks.text: Time: 5 minutes per page
chunks.heading: 1. Keyboard Test

chunks.text: Chrome's Lighthouse (Audits tab) has an Accessibility score. Run it and aim for 90+. It catches missing labels, low contrast, and missing ARIA.
chunks.heading: 2. Browser DevTools

chunks.text: Time: 1 minute per page
chunks.heading: 2. Browser DevTools

chunks.text: A free browser extension that finds accessibility issues and explains how to fix them. Much more detailed than Lighthouse.
chunks.heading: 3. axe DevTools Extension

chunks.text: Time: 2-3 minutes per page
chunks.heading: 3. axe DevTools Extension

chunks.text: Try VoiceOver (macOS: Cmd+F5), NVDA (Windows, free), or TalkBack (Android). Listen to your site — does it make sense?
chunks.heading: 4. Screen Reader

chunks.text: Time: 10-15 minutes (first time)
chunks.heading: 4. Screen Reader

chunks.text: Bookmark this quick-reference checklist for every page you build:
chunks.heading: Accessibility Checklist

chunks.text: Page has one <h1> and headings don't skip levels (h1 → h2 → h3)
chunks.heading: Structure

chunks.text: Page uses <main> , <nav> , <header> , <footer> landmarks
chunks.heading: Structure

chunks.text: Page has a <title> that describes the content
chunks.heading: Structure

chunks.text: Language is set: <html lang="en">
chunks.heading: Structure

chunks.text: All tags have alt text (or alt="" for decorative images)
chunks.heading: Images & Media

chunks.text: Icons used as buttons have aria-label
chunks.heading: Images & Media

chunks.text: Videos have captions or transcripts
chunks.heading: Images & Media

chunks.text: All interactive elements are reachable via keyboard (Tab / Shift+Tab)
chunks.heading: Interaction

chunks.text: Focus is visible on all focusable elements
chunks.heading: Interaction

chunks.text: Clickable elements use <button> or <a> , not <div>
chunks.heading: Interaction

chunks.text: Escape closes modals, dropdowns, and overlays
chunks.heading: Interaction

chunks.text: Every input has a visible <label>
chunks.heading: Forms

chunks.text: Required fields are indicated (not just with color)
chunks.heading: Forms

chunks.text: Error messages use aria-describedby and role="alert"
chunks.heading: Forms

chunks.text: Text contrast is at least 4.5:1 (check with DevTools)
chunks.heading: Color & Visual

chunks.text: Information isn't conveyed by color alone
chunks.heading: Color & Visual

chunks.text: Site is usable when zoomed to 200%
chunks.heading: Color & Visual

chunks.text: Learn how to boost page performance by deferring section contents until they are scrolled into view.
chunks.heading: Global Lazy Loading

chunks.text: Customize Vanduo's look by overriding CSS variables, and ensure contrast ratios stay accessible.
chunks.heading: CSS Variables & Theming

documents.title: Global Lazy Loading
headings: Step 3 The Simplest Usage: HTML Attributes
documents.bodyText: This guide walks you through using VanduoLazyLoad to build pages that load content on demand. Instead of shipping all HTML upfront, sections are fetched and injected only when they scroll into view — giving users a fast first paint and a smooth reading experience.. A documentation site can have hundreds of kilobytes of HTML across dozens of sections. Sending it all on the first request is wasteful — most users never scroll to the bottom. Lazy loading solves this by splitting content into small partials that are fetched on demand.. Ship only what's visible. The browser renders the page while the rest is pending.. Users who don't scroll never download off-screen content.. Each section is an independent HTML partial — easy to author and maintain separately.. VanduoLazyLoad is part of the Vanduo Framework and is included automatically in the standard bundle. If you're loading scripts individually, include it after the main framework files:. <!-- Option A: Full IIFE bundle (already includes lazy-load) --> < script src = "vanduo.min.js" ></ script > <!-- Option B: Individual scripts --> < script src = "js/utils/helpers.js" ></ script > < script src = "js/vanduo.js" ></ script > < script src = "js/components/lazy-load.js" ></ script > Step 2 Create HTML Partials Each lazy-loaded section is a standalone HTML fragment — just the content, no <html> or <body> wrapper. The root element should have a matching id so scroll-spy and anchor linking work correctly.. < section id = "intro" > < h2 >Introduction</ h2 > < p >This content was loaded lazily.</ p > </ section > Step 3 The Simplest Usage: HTML Attributes Add data-vd-lazy pointing to your partial, and optionally data-vd-lazy-placeholder for the loading state. Call Vanduo.init() once and everything is wired automatically.. < body > <!-- This section loads immediately (it's visible on page load) --> < div data-vd-lazy = "./sections/intro.html" data-vd-lazy-placeholder = "skeleton" > </ div > <!-- Below-fold section — fetched when user scrolls here --> < div data-vd-lazy = "./sections/details.html" data-vd-lazy-placeholder = "spinner" > </ div > < script >Vanduo.init();</ script > </ body > How it works: Vanduo.init() calls VanduoLazyLoad.init() , which scans the DOM for [data-vd-lazy] elements, shows the placeholder immediately, then uses IntersectionObserver to trigger the fetch when the element enters the viewport. Placeholder Options Value Result "skeleton" (default) Animated skeleton card with shimmer and four-color spinner overlay "spinner" Centered four-color spinner, minimal height Any HTML string Your custom HTML rendered while loading Step 4 Programmatic Usage For dynamic UIs where elements are created at runtime, use the JS API directly:. // High-level: section fetcher const el = document.getElementById( 'section-container' ); VanduoLazyLoad.loadSection( './sections/heavy.html' , el, { placeholder: 'skeleton' , onLoaded: (container) => { console.log( 'Section ready!' , container); }, onError: (err) => { console.error( 'Could not load section:' , err.message); } }); // Low-level: generic observe const img = document.querySelector( 'img[data-src]' ); VanduoLazyLoad.observe(img, (target) => { target.src = target.dataset.src; // swap src when visible }, { threshold: 0.1 }); API Reference Methods Method Parameters Description observe(el, cb, opts?) el : Element cb : (el: Element) => void opts.threshold : 0–1 opts.rootMargin : CSS string Fires cb once when el enters viewport, then unobserves unobserve(el) el : Element Stops observing a specific element unobserveAll() — Stops all active observations loadSection(url, el, opts?) url : string el : Element opts.placeholder : string opts.threshold : 0–1 opts.rootMargin : CSS string opts.onLoaded : (el) => void opts.onError : (err) => void Fetches an HTML partial and injects it into el when the element enters the viewport init() — Wires all [data-vd-lazy] elements in the DOM Events Event Fired on When lazysection:loading containerEl Immediately after placeholder is shown lazysection:loaded containerEl After content is injected successfully lazysection:error containerEl After a fetch failure (404, network error, etc.) Security note: loadSection() rejects any URL that resolves to a different origin. Only same-origin and relative URLs are allowed. Cross-origin requests are blocked silently with a console error. Listening to Events All events bubble from the container element, so you can listen globally or on the target:. // Global listeners (bubble from any lazy container) document.addEventListener( 'lazysection:loading' , (e) => { console.log( 'Fetching:' , e.detail.url); }); document.addEventListener( 'lazysection:loaded' , (e) => { console.log( 'Loaded:' , e.detail.url, 'into' , e.target); // Re-run analytics, highlight code, etc. }); document.addEventListener( 'lazysection:error' , (e) => { console.error( 'Failed:' , e.detail.url, e.detail.error); // Show a retry button, report to Sentry, etc. }); Real-world Patterns Lazy Images Use observe() to swap data-src → src when images enter view — native browser lazy loading without the loading="lazy" attribute.. Initialize chart libraries only when the chart container scrolls into view — avoids unnecessary JS execution on content users never see.. Place a sentinel element at the bottom of a list. When it enters view, fetch the next page and append it — infinite scroll without a framework.. Use observe() to add an is-visible class when elements enter view, triggering CSS transitions for fade-in, slide-up, or reveal effects.. Ensure your lazily loaded content is accessible to all users, including screen readers and keyboard navigation.. Quick reference for spacing, typography, colors, and layout utilities you can apply to any element.. How it works: Vanduo.init() calls VanduoLazyLoad.init() , which scans the DOM for [data-vd-lazy] elements, shows the placeholder immediately, then uses IntersectionObserver to trigger the fetch when the element enters the viewport.. Security note: loadSection() rejects any URL that resolves to a different origin. Only same-origin and relative URLs are allowed. Cross-origin requests are blocked silently with a console error.

chunks.text: This guide walks you through using VanduoLazyLoad to build pages that load content on demand. Instead of shipping all HTML upfront, sections are fetched and injected only when they scroll into view — giving users a fast first paint and a smooth reading experience.
chunks.heading: Global Lazy Loading Guide

chunks.text: A documentation site can have hundreds of kilobytes of HTML across dozens of sections. Sending it all on the first request is wasteful — most users never scroll to the bottom. Lazy loading solves this by splitting content into small partials that are fetched on demand.
chunks.heading: Why Lazy Loading?

chunks.text: Ship only what's visible. The browser renders the page while the rest is pending.
chunks.heading: Faster First Paint

chunks.text: Users who don't scroll never download off-screen content.
chunks.heading: Less Bandwidth

chunks.text: Each section is an independent HTML partial — easy to author and maintain separately.
chunks.heading: Composable Sections

chunks.text: VanduoLazyLoad is part of the Vanduo Framework and is included automatically in the standard bundle. If you're loading scripts individually, include it after the main framework files:
chunks.heading: Step 1 Include VanduoLazyLoad

chunks.text: Each lazy-loaded section is a standalone HTML fragment — just the content, no <html> or <body> wrapper. The root element should have a matching id so scroll-spy and anchor linking work correctly.
chunks.heading: Step 2 Create HTML Partials

chunks.text: Add data-vd-lazy pointing to your partial, and optionally data-vd-lazy-placeholder for the loading state. Call Vanduo.init() once and everything is wired automatically.
chunks.heading: Step 3 The Simplest Usage: HTML Attributes

chunks.text: "skeleton" (default) — Animated skeleton card with shimmer and four-color spinner overlay
chunks.heading: Placeholder Options

chunks.text: "spinner" — Centered four-color spinner, minimal height
chunks.heading: Placeholder Options

chunks.text: Any HTML string — Your custom HTML rendered while loading
chunks.heading: Placeholder Options

chunks.text: For dynamic UIs where elements are created at runtime, use the JS API directly:
chunks.heading: Step 4 Programmatic Usage

chunks.text: observe(el, cb, opts?) — el : Element cb : (el: Element) => void opts.threshold : 0–1 opts.rootMargin : CSS string
chunks.heading: Methods

chunks.text: unobserve(el) — el : Element
chunks.heading: Methods

chunks.text: unobserveAll() — —
chunks.heading: Methods

chunks.text: loadSection(url, el, opts?) — url : string el : Element opts.placeholder : string opts.threshold : 0–1 opts.rootMargin : CSS string opts.onLoaded : (el) => void opts.onError : (err) => void
chunks.heading: Methods

chunks.text: init() — —
chunks.heading: Methods

chunks.text: lazysection:loading — containerEl
chunks.heading: Events

chunks.text: lazysection:loaded — containerEl
chunks.heading: Events

chunks.text: lazysection:error — containerEl
chunks.heading: Events

chunks.text: All events bubble from the container element, so you can listen globally or on the target:
chunks.heading: Listening to Events

chunks.text: Use observe() to swap data-src → src when images enter view — native browser lazy loading without the loading="lazy" attribute.
chunks.heading: Lazy Images

chunks.text: Initialize chart libraries only when the chart container scrolls into view — avoids unnecessary JS execution on content users never see.
chunks.heading: Lazy Charts

chunks.text: Place a sentinel element at the bottom of a list. When it enters view, fetch the next page and append it — infinite scroll without a framework.
chunks.heading: Paginated Content

chunks.text: Use observe() to add an is-visible class when elements enter view, triggering CSS transitions for fade-in, slide-up, or reveal effects.
chunks.heading: Scroll Animations

chunks.text: Ensure your lazily loaded content is accessible to all users, including screen readers and keyboard navigation.
chunks.heading: Accessibility Essentials

chunks.text: Quick reference for spacing, typography, colors, and layout utilities you can apply to any element.
chunks.heading: Utilities Cheat Sheet
